NVIDIA disclosed in an 8-K filed after the close on April 15 that the U.S. government informed the company on April 9 that a license is now required to export its H20 integrated circuits — and any products achieving the H20's memory bandwidth or interconnect bandwidth — to China (including Hong Kong and Macau) and D:5 countries. On April 14, the government told NVIDIA the license requirement will be in effect for the indefinite future. The stated rationale is the risk that the products may be used in, or diverted to, supercomputers in China.

{"results": [{"chunk_id": "hyperscaler_capex::000", "title": "Hyperscaler capex commentary (MSFT/GOOG/META Q1 calls)", "published_at": "2025-05-02", "score": 19.556, "excerpt": "# Hyperscaler capex commentary (MSFT/GOOG/META Q1 calls)

The March-quarter earnings round from the big cloud buyers — Alphabet (April 24), Microsoft and Meta (April 30), and Amazon (May 1) — settled, at least for now, the question hanging over AI infrastructure names since the winter's data-center-lease scare: the 2025 budgets are intact, and one of them went up.

Meta raised its full-year 2025 capital-expenditure outlook to $64–72 billion, from $60–65 billion previously, citing additional data-center investments to support AI and higher expected costs of infrastructure hardware. Mark Zuckerberg framed the spend around AI now touching nearly every Meta product.

The bull case for NVIDIA needs only one assumption: that hyperscaler capital spending keeps compounding. The bear case is that we are watching the top of an investment cycle, and the warning lights are already blinking.

Start with the lease checks. TD Cowen's supply-chain work found Microsoft walked away from data-center projects totaling roughly two gigawatts of capacity across the U.S. and Europe over six months — driven, in their read, by a decision not to support incremental OpenAI training workloads. args.reason: Comprehensive evidence gathered: (1) Street preview with consensus ($43.3B rev, $0.88 non-GAAP EPS) and five key questions for May 28 print; (2) H20 export-control details — $5.5B charge flagged in April 15 8-K, license required indefinitely, open questions on how much hits Q1 and what guidance assumes; (3) Blackwell ramp — $11B Blackwell revenue in Q4 FY25, fastest product ramp ever, 3.6M GPU orders from top-4 cloud providers, GB300 transition in H2 2025; (4) Hyperscaler capex — $300B+ combined 2025 capex from MSFT/GOOG/META/AMZN, Meta raised guidance to $64-72B, demand side intact; (5) Bear case — capex digestion risk for FY2027, Microsoft data-center lease cancellations (~2GW), customer concentration (10%+ direct customers), GPU depreciation treadmill vs thin AI app revenue; (6) Price action — stock recovered from $94.18 April 4 low to $132.64 (May 22), +40.8% off bottom but -2% from recent $135.38 peak on May 19, showing momentum stalling into the print.
